Larry Wilson's company, sales training company, I can't think of the name now, but they did some research and in controlled tests, and I don't know how valid the tests were, but I buy the results, so I'm going to say the tests are valid. So are 50% more likely to buy something to accept a purchase if the paperwork is partially filled out for them in advance than if the paperwork is blank to start with? Well, once you hear it, of course it makes perfect sense, right? Especially if it's been filled out by hand and the paperwork is worthless, if they don't use it, now they feel like guilty and there's a sense of obligation to use the paperwork because somebody went to all the trouble to fill out the paperwork form as opposed to starting with a blank thing. So sales people have lots of ways that they can take control, right? So in coaching meetings, group environments, relatively small group, you can exercise control by place, right? Do they come to you? Do you go to them? Do you have it in a neutral location? What is a neutral location like? How is the seating arrangement made, etc. So for example, in my platinum group, I've had as few as 12. I've had as many as 20. And this year I think we have 21 coming to the meetings, okay? At 12, at 15, at 16, you can do all that in two days with a boardroom setup. Everybody's sitting at a table, everybody takes their turn in talks, everybody joins in. You don't play traffic cop enough to get through that. At 20 people, you can't do it. You won't be able to exercise enough control. So the platinum meeting done with the bigger number, everybody's at a boardroom table or a horseshoe table, but I'm up in front with a stool and a table. There's an extra stool and really we're doing hot seats. Each person comes up, we do their hot seat, they go back into town. It gives me more control over the schedule, the person, the interaction. It's easier for me to play cop. Now I'd rather do it the other way, but I got too many people, so now I got to do it in the most effective way for the number of people that I got. Rules. You've got to have rules. Everybody's got to know the rules. And by the way, you can't tell them the rules at the beginning of the year and then forget it. They got a lot on their minds and remembering the rules isn't one of them. It's real low on their priority list. Also the more successful the people are that you have in a coaching environment with you, of course, the less likely they are to follow rules because one of the reasons they're successful is because they don't follow rules. And now you're trying to get them to follow rules. It's, you know, we add by the way, encouraging them not to follow rules and anything else they do, but to by God follow your rules. So the rules better be pretty damn clear and they better be reminded of them every single time. So I got people that have been in platinum for ten years. They still got a memo before the meeting, reminds them of the rules, you know, they can be here on time, don't be late, don't leave early, don't bring a cell phone, you got to do it. You got to do it. And if you do not have clear rules and you do not remind them of them, your coaching environment will disintegrate a little bit each time until you suddenly realize there's chaos. And putting that genie back in the bottle ain't easy. Order. You got to think through what's going to happen, like how many minutes this is supposed to take, how many minutes this is supposed to take. You kind of got to have a little schedule. You kind of got to know where you're supposed to be. And you got to enforce everything. And mostly, coaching, group coaching meetings, mostly you're about playing traffic cop. I mean they're about keeping one person from dominating the conversation over others, about encouraging the timid to get in the game. Gee, Stephen, you've been quiet for the last hour. You must have some thoughts about this. And then you shut up and now Stephen will talk. And so you got to get in. And you got to be aware, oh shoot, Stephen hadn't been involved for the last two hours. Well, Stephen ain't going to be here next year if Stephen isn't involved. Now if you make him involved too much, then you won't keep him either. Because different people want to be involved at different levels. But he's got to be involved a little bit. He's got to have his moment in the sun a little bit. He's got to get to give his opinions a little bit. So you're traffic copping. You're keeping anybody from dominating. You're keeping an argument from turning into a full blown fight where nobody likes anybody anymore.
